reviews.review_msg: Joe is an extremely energetic professor with a good taste in fashion. His lectures are half informative and half group activities. With his course layout, you can learn anything you want as long as you use cognitive theory to explain what you're doing.
This course shouldn't be viewed as a GPA booster. If you want to formalize your understanding of how to learn and can commit to it, you will do fine.

lectures are well put and well executed, really good at explaining. The only downside is the mastery quizzes are worth 73% of your grade, and in order to get full marks you need 90% and above, and half marks is 83% and above. Mastering them isn't hard, just make flashcards and MEMORIZE them, it's not heavy concept and is easy to memorize.
In case my first rating didn't go through, check out "A COGS 110 Rant/Reflection" on the SFU Reddit for my thoughts on COGS 110 with Joe Thompson for the Spring 2019 Semester.

Great prof, he is always available for help outside of class. Lectures were at 8:30 and not have I once felt bored or tired. Makes the concepts super clear and easy to understand. The course however, is marked based off mastery (worth 72% of your grade and you must achieve a 90% of higher for full marks on tests. A very stressful class, great prof

I really enjoyed taking a class with Yves, there is no textbook and he very clearly explains topics and ideas and I find from the way he sets up his class makes learning so easy. His lectures are short, concise and clear. Mastery tests are not as scary as they seem and he gives you multiple opportunities to master each topic! Highly recommend.

elite lecturer, and very organised

Very difficult to do well in the class as his marking is so strict. I understand the purpose, but felt it was unrealistic. 

Wish I had taken this class in my first year bc I learned so much about how to learn
BEST PROF EVER! lots of studying but it was only like 15-30 minutes max a day, I learned so much. so good 😊

The whole course is based on mastery grading tests and making flash cards ever class. You learn nothing about cognitive science itself just worrying about buying flash cards every week. He does not respond to emails ever so be prepared to never skip class. Overall just lots of time needs to be devoted to this class just to pass. Not awesome.

You have to fully understand 100% of the material to at least pass the mastery tests as they aren't just basic questions, you have to apply your knowledge and think through the questions carefully. Almost finished with a bad grade but thankfully professor was nice enough to make final easy. Also, learning logs are an easy 100%

Prof. Blair's COGS110 is challenging but worthwhile. The material is engaging, and his lectures are entertaining. While attendance is mandatory, the mastery-based assessments allow multiple attempts. unstuckstudy was helpful for reviewing concepts and preparing for quizzes. Overall, a rewarding course if you're willing to put in the effort.

Dr. Blair is one of my favorite professors I've had so far! He makes the lectures engaging and makes time for students to take down notes. COGS 110 is a very structured course (as in he will tell you what to and how to study).

The grading is very narrow but if you study and pay attention in lectures you will do okay. You need to understand the concepts. Dr. Blair is very nice and tries to make the lectures enjoyable he wants his students to do well and he tells you exactly how you can do that but it is challenging if you don't prioritize it properly.
If you don't want to be studying all the time, do not take this course.

Do not take this course if you aren't prepared to do the work (15-30min a day). Dr Blair will give you the tools and opportunities to learn how to properly succeed in the class. His lectures keep you engaged and if you take it seriously, the course content is very, very useful in future classes. Highly recommmend

As Dr. Blair 'created' this course himself, I wouldn't recommend taking it from another prof. This course is based on mult. choice mastery tests, with multiple attempts each. While the material was very useful, Dr. Blair tends to drone on a bit and can fall a bit behind schedule. Very accessible and helpful, study hard and it's a easy course.
The worst course I have ever taken at SFU. He doesn't curve anything and the quizzes are really hard if you don't get 11/12 you won't pass that topic.

Funny and engaging prof. He teaches really well and the material is interesting, but he talks really quickly. He'll literally tell you how to study. If you don't do things right from the very beginning, you'll struggle a lot. Otherwise, it'll go pretty smoothly and you'll learn actually interesting things that you can apply to your daily life.

Mark cares for his students' learning and has engaging lectures. Course is easy if you put in the effort. Do your flashcards and listen in class. He records his lectures but doesn't post his slides. Pay attention to what he emphasizes because it'll show up on the quizzes. Quizzes are mastery-graded, so you can only get 100% if you get 11/12 or up.

Stopped attending lectures because he spends so much time going over the answers to practice questions that he puts in his own slides. The worst I recorded was 10 minutes spent trying to figure out and explain the answers OF ONE SLIDE, he wasn't sure if what he had written made sense. Not good at explaining concepts, skip and teach yourself.

his lectures are boring and his slides are concept heavy, but it isn't that hard to understand them without listening to the lecture. the grading criteria is clear and straightforward and the course content can be a bit dry sometimes. it would be harder to fail than to pass this class.

His lectures were very boring and I found it hard to follow along sometimes. If you asked a question he would not directly answer it which is bit annoying, other than that he is a fair and clear grader. as long as you watch the lectures and follow the material you cant fail this class

60 percent quizzes and 40 percent exams. His lectures are boring but straightforward. The concepts are easy to understand as long as you attend lectures which 95 percent of students don't. His lectures are blended so you can listen online or go in person. As long as you do the quizzes and practice a little you'll be fine for the course
He does his lectures in person and on zoom and then posts the recordings afterwards. His lectures are straightforward. You can watch the recordings on 2x speed and still understand everything.

Shawn is a great professor. He cares a lot about what he teaches. That being said I didn't show up to most lectures and still was able to do well. Everying is recorded and he does live zooms with optional attendance. You can't just read the slides wihtout watching the zooms as they are concept heavy with little text.

Shawn is a little different but once I got to know how he asks questions it was easy. I loved learning the material as it can be applied to life and studying, and it was a pretty easy class. Do the quizzes and you will pass, the 2 exams are a bonus.

meh lectures. Don't even bother showing up. lol. Quizzes are online and easy, you get 3 tries and open book. 2 Tests, worth 20% each. all MCQ. Prof also makes some typos in slides. Overall just a filler elective course if you need to take it with this prof.

Professor Tan's lectures feel useless and can sometimes be very all over the place. He also becomes extremely passive aggressive and downright not helpful when you email him or ask any questions and completely lacks the ability to adapt to things he doesn't expect.

Professor Tan wasn't the greatest professor. I believe he was new to teaching at SFU and wasn't completely organized in his lectures. The main issue was that he had a thick accent and it made it difficult to understand what he was saying. The content was quite interesting but Shawn made it hard to be passionate about the material.
